query: can whole genome sequencing accurately identify pathogenic variants in mitochondrial diseases?

retrieved_docs.title: Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease -- Evaluation -- Genetic Testing
retrieved_docs.content: NGS of the entire exome may find the majority of the disease-causing genes. Targeted panel gene testing typically includes a limited number of genes based on the patient's phenotype. It has better technical performance, easier data analysis, fewer incidental findings, lower cost, and is easier to adopt in small laboratories. However, targeted panels may fail to identify novel gene associations. [85] NGS is the preferred technique for establishing the genetic diagnosis in CMT once CNVs or mutations in common genes are excluded. Even with whole-exome sequencing, about 40% of patients remain without a genetic diagnosis. NGS is negative in cases of mutations in genes that are unidentified or not described in the setting of CMT. NGS may miss mutations in the untranslated region (UTR), promoter, and other non-exonic regions. NGS is not sensitive to identifying CNVs or epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational changes. Functional analysis is essential to ascribe pathogenicity when NGS identifies novel variants. [86]

retrieved_docs.title: Medium-Chain Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency -- Evaluation -- Molecular testing for genotypic mutations
retrieved_docs.content: Molecular genetic testing, utilizing whole DNA or exosome sequencing to detect mutations in the ACADM gene, is highly sensitive for detecting mutant variants. However, the clinical significance of this diagnostic tool for MCADD is unclear. To date, molecular testing has identified more than fifty genetic variants of uncertain significance in the ACADM gene. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease -- Evaluation -- Genetic Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing has evolved with the development of NGS technology, which is capable of sequencing the entire human genome, its protein-coding sequences, or specific genes of interest in parallel. [83] The yield of NGS depends on the gene panel used. The exome refers to the complete set of protein-coding regions in the human genome. Although it constitutes only 1% of the total genomic content, it contains more than 85% of the functional variations. Whole-exome sequencing evaluates the entire protein-coding region in an unselected or unbiased manner. Whole-genome sequencing evaluates both coding and noncoding regions. Some laboratories distinguish between clinical exome and whole-exome sequencing; the former refers to a set of genes implicated in most human diseases. [84]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Mutation -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, targeted sequencing, whole-exome sequencing (WES), and whole-genome sequencing (WGS) are the three main sequencing approaches used in the clinical setting to evaluate for the presence of a rare or novel genetic variant. These techniques are often collectively referred to as next-generation sequencing (NGS). The first of these, gene panels, evaluate a certain, specific number of genes or regions within genes that have been reported to contain mutations contributory to a specific disease or group of diseases. [83] Gene panels are economical, rapid, and flexible but may vary between laboratories regarding the included genes and their reasoning for inclusion. This makes a comparison between different panels difficult. Furthermore, as the literature expands on certain diseases, panels must be updated routinely to capture newly described mutations. Additionally, many of the current panels are predominantly based on genetic analysis of individuals with European ancestry, limiting generalizability to other groups. In contrast, WES and WGS are both more extensive regarding the extent to which they sequence the genome. WES involves the sequencing of protein-coding regions of the genome, which can identify genetic mutations not yet associated with specific pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: In axonal neuropathies, the patient should first be tested for mutations in MFN2 . Alternatively, they can undergo targeted gene sequencing or whole-exome sequencing. Establishing the genetic diagnosis is crucial for ongoing genetic counseling, reproductive planning, and considering the patient for potential new therapies in research. Patients with CMT should undergo monitoring for comorbid conditions. Patients with CMT should avoid using drugs that worsen neuropathy. The interprofessional healthcare team should offer patient education and counseling, regular follow-up, emphasize rehabilitation measures, and provide access to therapeutic trials.

retrieved_docs.title: Gitelman Syndrome -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: In the near future, whole-exome and whole-genome sequencing, followed by targeted analysis, are more likely to become the genetic tests of preference. Genetic counseling and informed consent should always precede these genetic screening tests. [43] With the increasing availability of functional studies and new-population–based genetic data, the classification of variants may be changing. The perception about previous disease-associated genetic variants may change, their pathogenicity could be challenged, and, conversely, variants with unknown significance may be confirmed as pathogenic. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Char Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing plays an important role in confirming the diagnosis of Char syndrome, identifying causative mutations, and for genetic counseling. Genetic testing can identify mutations in the gene (heterogenic pathogenic variant). [16] [17] Different modalities of molecular genetic testing can include gene-targeted sequencing and whole-exome sequencing. In individuals presenting with symptoms suggestive of Char syndrome, gene-targeted sequencing such as single gene testing and multi-gene panels are used, while in individuals where Char syndrome is not considered, whole-exome sequencing or gene panel testing is utilized. [1] [16] Selecting the most suitable testing requires careful analysis of clinical features, differential diagnosis, and available resources.

retrieved_docs.title: Kearns-Sayre Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Next-generation sequencing (NGS) of the mitochondrial DNA genome in peripheral blood leukocyte samples to identify deletions is the preferred diagnostic modality in cases of clinically suspected KSS. If blood tests for KSS are negative in patients who have a strong pretest probability of having KSS based on their phenotype, their muscle mtDNA should be sequenced as sometimes tissue-specific mutations can be missed and low levels of heteroplasmy in the blood can lead to false-negative results. In many patients with KSS, there is an elevation of protein in the cerebrospinal fluid thus lumbar puncture with the analysis of its content should be performed.

retrieved_docs.title: Lafora Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A case study collected Lafora disease cases for diagnosis and confirmed the genetic spectrum of a family. Furthermore, whole-exome sequencing of the pedigree was conducted for molecular diagnosis. Results showed that a novel biallelic compound heterozygous c.333dupC chr6-18122504 (p.[Gly112ArgfsTer44]) and c.612dupT chr6-18122225 (p.[Gly205Trpfs*29]) mutation in the NHLRC1 gene was elucidated in the progressive myoclonic epilepsy of Lafora pedigree. This means that genetic analysis was helpful in the diagnosis of Lafora disease. Moreover, genetic analysis with alternative tissue biopsy was recommended for patients and close relatives. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: McArdle Disease (Glycogen Storage Disease Type 5)  -- Evaluation -- Additional Diagnostic Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Muscle biopsy (vastus lateralis or biceps brachialis) shows periodic acid Schiff-positive vacuoles of high glycogen content and absence of myophosphorylase. Genetic testing includes options of specific mutation analysis (most commonly R50X in white population), next-generation PYGM gene sequencing panels, myopathy panels, or whole-exome sequencing for particular glycogen-storage diseases. Typically, patients are diagnosed based on whether they are homozygous or compound heterozygous for PYGM pathogenic mutations. A study aimed to formulate a less invasive approach to diagnose the disease and determine PYGM expression in white blood cells using antibodies.

retrieved_docs.title: Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease -- Evaluation -- Genetic Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Sanger sequencing is the standard in molecular genetic diagnosis. However, it has limitations in diagnosing CMT due to the large number of possible genetic variations. Mutations in genes other than PMP22 , MPZ , GJB1, and MFN2 are rare, making it challenging to prioritize and conduct sequential analysis of all the possible CMT genes by Sanger sequencing. This approach is both time-consuming and expensive.

retrieved_docs.title: Kearns-Sayre Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: CPEO must be differentiated from other disorders associated with ophthalmoplegia. These include: Isolated chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia (CPEO) Oculopharyngeal muscular dystrophy Myotonic dystrophy Isolated oculopharyngeal myopathy Mendelian chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia (CPEO) associated with multiple deletions of mtDNA such as POLG

retrieved_docs.title: Lafora Disease -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic analysis helps diagnose Lafora disease if consanguinity or Lafora bodies are observed. A case was reported on siblings with progressive myoclonus epilepsy whose parents were not consanguineous. Their clinical symptoms were typical of Lafora disease. However, skin biopsies were negative for Lafora bodies. Also, whole-exome sequencing revealed a recurrent homozygous frameshift variant in the NHLRC1 gene in the patients. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Molecular Genetics Testing -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Whole-exome sequencing or whole-genome sequencing tests analyze the bulk of an individual's DNA to find genetic variations. This approach is useful when a single-gene or panel testing has not provided a diagnosis or when the suspected condition or genetic cause is unclear. [15] This sequencing method is often more cost- and time-effective than performing multiple single gene or panel tests. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Dysdiadochokinesia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary: Inherited ataxias undergo screening through the clinical and family history before being sent to the geneticist for formal counseling and testing. DNA southern blot, RNA Northern blot, methylation assays, karyotyping, whole-genome sequencing

retrieved_docs.title: Myotonia Congenita -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A multigene panel is generally recommended as the initial approach. Such panels include CLCN1 and other genes of interest, notably SCN4A , which encodes the skeletal muscle sodium channel Nav1.4 and is relevant in the differential diagnosis. Testing should be performed in specialized centers where appropriate methodologies are used to evaluate variants of uncertain significance, correlate identified mutations with clinical features, and limit costs. Broader genomic methods, such as exome sequencing or mitochondrial sequencing, may be pursued if no causative variant is identified on panel testing. [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1439
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic disturbances that encompass specific conditions such as اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي and فرط البروتينات الشحمية. This category also includes the clinical concept ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) as one of its members, indicating a link between lipid abnormalities and early‑onset hyperglycemia. In classification systems, disorders of lipid metabolism are mapped to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ, a form of familial hyperlipidemia. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that the trait is transmitted through a single copy of the altered gene from an affected parent. Together, these relationships highlight how lipid metabolism disorders are interconnected with other metabolic disorders, specific disease entities, and genetic inheritance mechanisms, informing both diagnostic categorization and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 930
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant, with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy, type 1 is a hereditary cerebrovascular disorder that characteristically presents with a gait disorder (اضطراب المِشْيَة). The disease’s underlying vascular pathology leads to subcortical infarctions and diffuse white‑matter changes, which together disrupt motor pathways and result in abnormal walking patterns. Recognizing gait disturbance as a key manifestation of this specific type of cerebral arteriopathy aids clinicians in diagnosing the condition and guiding appropriate genetic counseling and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.content: diseases, genetic neuropathies, and distinct syndromes. This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the network of relationships portrays diabetes as a multifaceted condition that influences and is influenced by genetic abnormalities, neonatal presentations, limb and ocular complications, metabolic comorbidities, diagnostic testing, and medication safety, all of which are essential considerations for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. This highlights that the nerve is a common locus for diverse neuropathic processes, ranging from acute hemorrhagic injury to inflammatory, toxic, and genetic sensory neuropathies. Recognizing the nerve as the affected structure helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and tailor management strategies for patients presenting with these nerve‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 216

This integrated view supports comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care across rehabilitation and metabolic disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 669
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are recognized as a specific form of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and are also classified under hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). Within this category, elevated blood glucose and new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)) are considered member conditions, highlighting the close link between lipid abnormalities and early disturbances in carbohydrate metabolism. Laboratory results that are “above reference range” are interpreted as indicative of a disorder of lipid metabolism, underscoring the diagnostic relevance of lipid panels. The disorder “فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ” (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is mapped to the broader concept of disorders of lipid metabolism and is known to follow an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, indicating that a single copy of the pathogenic allele can transmit the condition. Moreover, carbohydrate metabolism disorders (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات) are positioned as a parent category to metabolic disorders (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي), reflecting the hierarchical organization of metabolic disease entities. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid metabolism disorders intersect with broader metabolic pathways, genetic inheritance, and clinical interpretation, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 560
retrieved_docs.content: targeted screenings, acute and chronic disease subtypes, genetic and toxicologic conditions, laboratory abnormalities, malignancies, trauma, and specialized organ disorders within a unified, multi‑lingual hierarchical structure that facilitates accurate coding, comprehensive patient assessment, and coordinated care across clinical and administrative settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: Autosomal dominant inheritance is the mode of transmission for فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (hyperlipidemia type IIa). This genetic pattern means that a single copy of the altered gene inherited from either parent is sufficient to cause the disorder. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is characterized by markedly elevated low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, which increase the risk of premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Because the condition follows an autosomal dominant pattern, it often appears in successive generations, and first‑degree relatives of an affected individual have a 50 % chance of inheriting the same lipid abnormality. Recognizing the autosomal dominant inheritance of فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is essential for family screening, early diagnosis, and implementation of lipid‑lowering therapies to reduce cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary disorder of nervous system is a broad category of genetic conditions affecting the nervous system, and شقيقة فالجية عائلية (familial hemiplegic migraine) is a specific instance within this category. شقيقة فالجية عائلية represents a hereditary form of migraine that is characterized by transient hemiplegia and other neurological symptoms, illustrating how a hereditary disorder of the nervous system can manifest as a distinct migraine subtype.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these distinct neonatal and genetic forms of diabetes are considered subtypes of diabetes mellitus. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting a general retinal disorder that can occur in diabetic patients, and more specifically with اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), a hallmark microvascular complication of diabetes. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية (end‑stage renal disease), demonstrating that chronic diabetes can progress to severe kidney failure. The relationship between اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ and مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية further underscores the shared microvascular pathology that links diabetic eye disease and diabetic kidney disease, highlighting the importance of early detection and integrated management of these complications in patients with any form of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2636
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “vrozené,” which denotes a congenital origin, can be applied to several disorders, indicating that they may be present from birth. It is an allowed qualifier for ناسور مَثانِيٌّ مَهْبِلِيّ, قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة, انسداد صفراوي خارج الكبد, فرط البروتينات الشحمية, and الْتِهابُ المثانة. Recognizing these conditions as congenital guides clinicians toward appropriate genetic evaluation, early diagnostic testing, and tailored management plans that address their lifelong nature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 370
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is linked to a broad spectrum of related disorders and complications. Transient neonatal forms of diabetes, including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 1 and DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2, as well as the syndrome of Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating they represent variants of the same metabolic disease. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with retinal disorders such as اضطراب في الشبكية and اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), and these retinal complications are further linked to end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). In turn, مرض السكري itself is clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease, highlighting the kidney as a major target organ. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1763
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic conditions that include specific entities such as فرط البروتينات الشحمية, which is recognized as a particular type of lipid metabolism disorder. Within this same category, the condition ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) is listed as a member, indicating that early‑onset diabetes is considered part of the spectrum of lipid metabolism abnormalities. The SMQ is further characterized by the clinical effect تأثير إدرار البول, reflecting the polyuric manifestation commonly seen in new‑onset hyperglycemia. Additionally, the disorder فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is mapped to the disorders of lipid metabolism group and is known to follow an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, highlighting a genetic basis for this specific hyperlipidemic condition. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid metabolism disorders can intersect with early diabetes presentations and hereditary factors, underscoring the importance of integrated assessment and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2796
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as a genetic factor (genetika) associated with several specific conditions, including اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ (excessive tearing), and فَرْطُ ضَغْطِ الدَّم العيني (increased intra‑ocular pressure). Recognizing this genetic linkage can aid clinicians in understanding the hereditary contributions to these disorders and may inform diagnostic evaluation and personalized management strategies.
